documents.title: Core Philosophy: Three-Tier Intensity Model
documents.content:
Training is NOT polarized (hard/easy only). We use a three-tier intensity model: hard, moderate, and easy. This is fundamental.

Hard sessions: Full effort workouts — intervals, race-specific work, hard tempos. These create the primary training stimulus. They should be placed strategically with proper recovery before and after.

Moderate sessions: 7 out of 10 effort. These are the backbone of aerobic development. They include aerobic support runs, moderate long runs, and sessions designed to help the body adapt to a stimulus WITHOUT overloading it. This applies to aerobic work, speed development, and aerobic power work alike. Moderate sessions allow adaptation without excessive fatigue.

Easy sessions: True recovery. Very easy on heart rate, low intensity, fully conversational by feel. After hard sessions, the body may be fatigued — muscularly, systemically — and needs an easy session to absorb the prior stimulus. Easy and moderate are NOT the same thing. A recovery run should be distinctly slower than a moderate aerobic support run.

The coach should never treat moderate efforts as 'junk miles' or push athletes toward strict polarized training. Moderate work is intentional and serves aerobic development.

documents.title: Core Philosophy: Canova-Inspired Reverse Engineering
documents.content:
Training plans should be built backward from the goal. Identify 2-3 key race-specific workouts the athlete needs to be able to execute before race day, then reverse-engineer the training block to build toward those sessions.

This is loosely based on Renato Canova's approach: the training block serves the final goal, not the other way around. We don't follow a rigid template — we identify what the athlete needs to DO on race day and construct the path to get there.

Earlier in the block: Focus on adaptation. Sessions should be moderate and achievable. The body needs time to adapt without being overloaded. Build the aerobic and structural foundation.

Later in the block: Intensity increases. Harder efforts are introduced progressively. The athlete should arrive at key workouts fresh enough to execute them well, because the program has set them up with appropriate recovery.

The training block is not about accumulating fatigue — it's about building capacity. If the athlete can't execute the key sessions because they're buried in fatigue, the program has failed.

documents.title: Core Philosophy: Flexible Training, Not Rigid Blocks
documents.content:
Training should be fluid and flexible, adjusted day-by-day based on how the athlete feels. Never push athletes into a rigid training block.

The body gives signals: fatigue, soreness trending toward injury, poor sleep, inadequate fueling. When these signals appear, the program must adapt — take days off, swap to easy days, remove runs entirely. Ignoring these signals leads to injury and stagnation.

Adaptation happens over months, not weeks. On a week-to-week basis, be very cognizant of fatigue factors. But over a 6-month cycle, the body can adapt to dramatically more than where it started. Trust the long arc.

Not every week needs to be heavily stacked. Some training sessions need to be harder than others. The athlete needs excellent recovery before hard, extensive sessions (long run workouts, long tempo/threshold sessions). The program should ensure the athlete is set up to absorb those stimuli.

If an athlete is doing hard workouts and not recovering well, their body won't make proper adaptations. More is not better — better recovery enables better adaptation.

documents.title: Core Philosophy: Volume and Distance Specificity
documents.content:
Volume prescription depends heavily on the target event.

For longer events (half marathon, marathon): Global volume becomes the priority. 60-70 miles per week will get a marathoner in significantly better shape than 40 miles per week. Long runs must be prioritized — both in frequency and in their proportion of total volume. The long run and overall aerobic support volume are the primary drivers of fitness for these distances.

For 10K to half marathon: Long runs still matter significantly. Volume supports aerobic development. A 15-mile long run (for example) provides critical aerobic sport-specific support.

For shorter events (mile, 5K): Prioritize aerobic power development and speed endurance. Global volume is still important but the training emphasis shifts toward quality speed work and the ability to sustain fast paces.

Volume should not be rigidly consistent and high. The body adapts on a month-over-month basis, not week-over-week. Some weeks may need to be lighter for recovery. Volume builds over training cycles — progressive overload across months, with recovery weeks built in.

Both the single session (the long run, the key workout) AND the holistic training block (weekly mileage, overall aerobic work) matter. Neither should be neglected.

documents.title: Core Philosophy: Aerobic Support and Development
documents.content:
Aerobic support is a priority across all event distances. This means:

- Threshold runs: Sustained efforts near lactate threshold to extend the athlete's ability to hold pace.
- Moderate runs: Aerobic endurance runs at moderate effort (7/10) that build and maintain the aerobic base. These are NOT recovery runs — they serve a distinct training purpose.
- Long runs: Extended aerobic sessions that develop fat oxidation, capillary density, and mental durability.

Aerobic support should be consistent over time. It's not something you do in a 'base phase' and then abandon. Throughout the training block, aerobic work continues to underpin everything else.

The difference between easy and moderate is critical:
- Easy/recovery: Truly easy. Conversational. Low heart rate. Exists to recover from hard work.
- Moderate/aerobic support: Purposeful effort. Slightly faster than easy. Builds the aerobic engine. Think long run pace, steady-state aerobic work. This is where long-term aerobic development happens.

Athletes who only run hard or easy miss the massive middle ground where aerobic development thrives.

documents.title: Core Philosophy: Recovery and Adaptation
documents.content:
Recovery is not optional — it's where adaptation happens. The training stimulus breaks the body down; recovery is when it rebuilds stronger.

Key principles:
- Easy days after hard days must be genuinely easy. The body may be fatigued muscularly, neurologically, or systemically. Respect that.
- Days off are a tool, not a failure. When the body signals it needs rest (persistent fatigue, sleep disruption, trending toward injury), take the day off.
- Sleep and nutrition are recovery tools. If these are compromised, training load should decrease.
- Recovery needs vary by individual and by phase. During heavy training blocks, more recovery is needed. During lighter phases, the athlete can absorb more.

Signs that recovery is insufficient:
- Performances declining despite consistent training
- Persistent muscle soreness that doesn't resolve between sessions
- Elevated resting heart rate
- Loss of motivation or enjoyment
- Minor injuries or niggles that won't heal

The program should always set the athlete up so they arrive at hard sessions ready to execute. If they can't, the recovery between sessions is inadequate — either the easy days aren't easy enough, or there aren't enough of them.

documents.title: Core Philosophy: Session Setup and Sequencing
documents.content:
How sessions are sequenced matters as much as what the sessions are.

Before hard sessions: The athlete needs to arrive recovered and ready. This may mean an easy day, a day off, or a very light moderate session — depending on what came before. A hard workout done in a fatigued state teaches the body to run tired, not to run fast. That has its place occasionally, but should not be the norm.

After hard sessions: Easy recovery. The stimulus has been delivered — now let the body absorb it. Moderate work can return once the athlete has recovered.

Not every week is the same intensity: Some weeks are build weeks with harder sessions. Some are consolidation weeks with moderate work. Some are recovery weeks with reduced volume and intensity. The pattern should serve the training block's arc, not a rigid weekly template.

Hard sessions should be protected: If the athlete has a key long run or a key workout, the days surrounding it should be designed to maximize the quality of that session. Don't stack hard work around key sessions.

Training within yourself: The goal is long-term development, not proving fitness in training. Keep the body relatively protected. Sessions should be executed with control, not desperation.
